[[File:Transcranial magnetic stimulation.jpg|thumb|[[Transcranial magnetic stimulation]] uses magnetism to safely stimulate or inhibit parts of the brain.]]

Some research suggests that [[Transcranial magnetic stimulation|TMS]] can be used to manipulate the perception of authorship of a specific choice.<ref name="Walsh2005">{{cite book|author=Vincent Walsh|title=Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation: A Neurochronometrics of Mind|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rACRPwAACAAJ|year=2005|publisher=MIT Press|isbn=978-0-262-73174-4}}</ref> Experiments showed that neurostimulation could affect which hands people move, even though the subjective experience of will was intact. An early [[Transcranial magnetic stimulation|TMS]] study revealed that activation of one side of the [[neocortex]] could be used to bias the selection of one's opposite side hand in a forced-choice decision task.<ref name="ammon">{{cite journal|doi=10.1136/jnnp.53.8.705|title=Transcranial magnetic stimulation can influence the selection of motor programmes|year=1990|last1=Ammon|first1=K.|last2=Gandevia|first2=S. C.|journal=Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry|volume=53|issue=8|pages=705–7|pmid=2213050|pmc=488179}}</ref> K. Ammon and S. C. Gandevia found that it was possible to influence which hand people move by stimulating [[frontal lobe|frontal]] regions that are involved in movement planning using [[transcranial magnetic stimulation]] in the left or right hemisphere of the brain.<ref name="ammon"/>

Right-handed people would normally choose to move their right hand 60% of the time, but when the right hemisphere was stimulated, they would instead choose their left hand 80% of the time (recall that the right hemisphere of the brain is responsible for the left side of the body, and the left hemisphere for the right). Despite the external influence on their decision-making, the subjects were apparently unaware of any influence, as when questioned they felt that their decisions appeared to be made in an entirely natural way.<ref name="ammon"/> In a follow-up experiment, [[Alvaro Pascual-Leone]] and colleagues found similar results, but also noted that the transcranial magnetic stimulation must occur within the motor area and within 200&nbsp;milliseconds, consistent with the time-course derived from the Libet experiments: with longer response times (between 200 and 1100 ms), magnetic stimulation had no effect on hand preference regardless of the site stimulated.<ref name="Brasil-NetoPascual-Leone1992">{{cite journal|doi=10.1136/jnnp.55.10.964|title=Focal transcranial magnetic stimulation and response bias in a forced-choice task|year=1992|last1=Brasil-Neto|first1=J. P.|last2=Pascual-Leone|first2=A.|last3=Valls-Sole|first3=J.|last4=Cohen|first4=L. G.|last5=Hallett|first5=M.|journal=Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry|volume=55|issue=10|pages=964–6|pmid=1431962|pmc=1015201}}</ref>

In late 2015, following a previous 2010 study,<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1073/pnas.1006223107|title=Transcranial magnetic stimulation of posterior parietal cortex affects decisions of hand choice|year=2010|last1=Oliveira|first1=Flavio T. P.|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America|volume=107|issue=41|pages=17751–17756|pmid=20876098|last2=Diedrichsen|first2=Jörn|last3=Verstynen|first3=Timothy|last4=Duque|first4=Julie|last5=Ivry|first5=Richard B.|doi-access=free|pmc=2955129 |bibcode=2010PNAS..10717751O }}</ref> both based on earlier investigations on both monkeys and humans, a team of researchers from the UK and the US published an article demonstrating similar findings. The researchers concluded that "motor responses and the choice of hand can be modulated using [[Transcranial direct-current stimulation|tDCS]]".<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1162/JOCN_A_00848|title=Transcranial Direct Current Stimulation of the Motor Cortex Biases Action Choice in a Perceptual Decision Task|year=2015|last1=Javadi|first1=Amir-Homayoun|journal=Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience|volume=27|issue=11|pages=2174–85|pmid=26151605|last2=Beyko|first2=Angeliki|last3=Walsh|first3=Vincent|pmc=4745131}}</ref> However, a different attempt by Y. H. Sohn ''et al.'' failed to replicate such results.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1136/jnnp.74.7.985|title=The effect of transcranial magnetic stimulation on movement selection|year=2003|last1=Sohn|first1=Y. H.|journal=Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry|volume=74|issue=7|pages=985–7|pmid=12810802|last2=Kaelin-Lang|first2=A.|last3=Hallett|first3=M.|pmc=1738563}}</ref>

=== Manipulating the perceived intention to move ===
Various studies indicate that the perceived intention to move (have moved) can be manipulated. Studies have focused on the pre-[[supplementary motor area]] (pre-SMA) of the brain, in which readiness potential indicating the beginning of a movement genesis has been recorded by EEG. In one study, directly stimulating the pre-SMA caused volunteers to report a feeling of intention, and sufficient stimulation of that same area caused physical movement.<ref name="Haggard, P. 2008"/> In a similar study, it was found that people with no visual awareness of their body can have their limbs be made to move without having any awareness of this movement, by stimulating [[premotor cortex|premotor]] brain regions. When their parietal cortices were stimulated, they reported an urge (intention) to move a specific limb (that they wanted to do so). Furthermore, stronger stimulation of the parietal cortex resulted in the illusion of having moved without having done so.<ref name="DesmurgetReilly2009">{{cite journal|last1=Desmurget|first1=M.|last2=Reilly|first2=K. T.|last3=Richard|first3=N.|last4=Szathmari|first4=A.|last5=Mottolese|first5=C.|last6=Sirigu|first6=A.|title=Movement Intention After Parietal Cortex Stimulation in Humans|journal=Science|volume=324|issue=5928|year=2009|pages=811–813|issn=0036-8075|doi=10.1126/science.1169896|pmid=19423830|bibcode=2009Sci...324..811D|s2cid=6555881}}</ref>

This suggests that awareness of an intention to move may literally be the "sensation" of the body's early movement, but certainly not the cause. Other studies have at least suggested that "The greater activation of the SMA, SACC, and parietal areas during and after execution of internally generated actions suggests that an important feature of internal decisions is specific neural processing taking place during and after the corresponding action. Therefore, awareness of intention timing seems to be fully established only after execution of the corresponding action, in agreement with the time course of neural activity observed here."<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.3389/neuro.09.014.2007|title=High-frequency oscillations in distributed neural networks reveal the dynamics of human decision making|year=2008|last1=Guggisberg|first1=Adrian G.|journal=Frontiers in Human Neuroscience|volume=1|pmid=18958227|last2=Dalal|first2=S. S.|last3=Findlay|first3=A. M.|last4=Nagarajan|first4=S. S.|page=14|pmc=2525986|doi-access=free}}</ref>

Another experiment involved an electronic [[ouija]] board where the device's movements were manipulated by the experimenter, while the participant was led to believe that they were entirely self-conducted.<ref name="WegnerWheatley1999">{{cite journal|last1=Wegner|first1=Daniel M.|last2=Wheatley|first2=Thalia|title=Apparent mental causation: Sources of the experience of will|journal=American Psychologist|volume=54|issue=7|year=1999|pages=480–492|issn=0003-066X|doi=10.1037/0003-066X.54.7.480|pmid=10424155|citeseerx=10.1.1.188.8271}}</ref> The experimenter stopped the device on occasions and asked the participant how much they themselves felt like they wanted to stop. The participant also listened to words in headphones, and it was found that if experimenter stopped next to an object that came through the headphones, they were more likely to say that they wanted to stop there. If the participant perceived having the thought at the time of the action, then it was assigned as intentional. It was concluded that a strong illusion of perception of causality requires: priority (we assume the thought must precede the action), consistency (the thought is about the action), and exclusivity (no other apparent causes or alternative hypotheses).<ref name="WegnerWheatley1999"/>

Hakwan C. Lau et al. set up an experiment where subjects would look at an analog-style clock, and a red dot would move around the screen. Subjects were told to click the mouse button whenever they felt the intention to do so. One group was given a [[transcranial magnetic stimulation]] (TMS) pulse, and the other was given a sham TMS. Subjects in the perceived intention condition were told to move the cursor to where it was when they felt the inclination to press the button. In the movement condition, subjects moved their cursor to where it was when they physically pressed the button. TMS applied over the pre-SMA after a participant performed an action shifted the perceived onset of the motor intention backward in time, and the perceived time of action execution forward in time. Results showed that the TMS was able to shift the perceived intention condition forward by 16&nbsp;ms, and shifted back by 14&nbsp;ms for the movement condition. Perceived intention could be manipulated up to 200&nbsp;ms after the execution of the spontaneous action, indicating that the perception of intention occurred after the executive motor movements. The results of three control studies suggest that this effect is time-limited, specific to modality, and also specific to the anatomical site of stimulation. The investigators conclude that the perceived onset of intention depends, at least in part, on neural activity that takes place after the execution of action.<ref name="LauRogers2007" /> Often it is thought that if free will were to exist, it would require intention to be the causal source of behavior. These results show that intention may not be the causal source of all behavior.<ref name="LauRogers2007" />

=== Related models ===
The idea that intention co-occurs with (rather than causes) movement is reminiscent of "forward models of [[motor control]]" (FMMC), which have been used to try to explain [[inner speech]]. FMMCs describe parallel circuits: movement is processed in parallel with other predictions of movement; if the movement matches the prediction, the feeling of agency occurs. FMMCs have been applied in other related experiments. Janet Metcalfe and her colleagues used an FMMC to explain how volunteers determine whether they are in control of a computer game task. On the other hand, they acknowledge other factors as well. The authors attribute feelings of agency to desirability of the results (see [[self-serving bias]]es) and top-down processing (reasoning and inferences about the situation).<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2010.05.009|title=Metacognition of agency across the lifespan|year=2010|last1=Metcalfe|first1=Janet|last2=Eich|first2=Teal S.|last3=Castel|first3=Alan D.|journal=Cognition|volume=116|issue=2|pages=267–282|pmid=20570251|s2cid=4051484}}</ref>

There is also a model, called [[epiphenomenalism]], that argues that conscious will is an illusion, and that consciousness is a [[by-product]] of physical states of the world. Others have argued that data such as the ''Bereitschaftspotential'' undermine epiphenomenalism for the same reason, that such experiments rely on a subject reporting the point in time at which a conscious experience and a conscious decision occurs, thus relying on the subject to be able to consciously perform an action. That ability would seem to be at odds with epiphenomenalism, which, according to [[Thomas Henry Huxley]], is the broad claim that consciousness is "completely without any power… as the steam-whistle which accompanies the work of a [[locomotive]] engine is without influence upon its machinery".<ref name="Flanagan19922">{{cite book|last=Flanagan|first=O.J.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yFOqQgAACAAJ|title=Consciousness Reconsidered|publisher=MIT Press|year=1992|isbn=978-0-262-56077-1|series=Bradford Books|page=131|lccn=lc92010057}}</ref>

=== Related brain disorders ===
Various brain disorders implicate the role of unconscious brain processes in decision-making tasks. [[Auditory hallucination|Auditory hallucinations]] produced by [[schizophrenia]] seem to suggest a divergence of will and behaviour.<ref name="Wegner2003" /> The left brain of people whose hemispheres have been disconnected has been observed to invent explanations for body movement initiated by the opposing (right) hemisphere, perhaps based on the assumption that their actions are consciously willed.<ref name="Gazzaniga1984">{{cite book|last1=Gazzaniga|first1=Michael S.|editor1-first=Michael S|editor1-last=Gazzaniga|title=The Cognitive Neurosciences|year=1984|doi=10.1007/978-1-4899-2177-2|isbn=978-1-4899-2179-6|s2cid=5763744}}</ref> Likewise, people with "[[alien hand syndrome]]" are known to conduct complex motor movements against their will.<ref name="GeschwindIacoboni1995">{{cite journal|last1=Geschwind|first1=D. H.|last2=Iacoboni|first2=M.|last3=Mega|first3=M. S.|last4=Zaidel|first4=D. W.|last5=Cloughesy|first5=T.|last6=Zaidel|first6=E.|author-link5=Timothy Cloughesy|title=Alien hand syndrome: Interhemispheric motor disconnection due to a lesion in the midbody of the corpus callosum|journal=Neurology|volume=45|issue=4|year=1995|pages=802–808|issn=0028-3878|doi=10.1212/WNL.45.4.802|pmid=7723974|s2cid=39196545}}</ref>

=== Neural models of voluntary action ===
A neural model for voluntary action proposed by Haggard comprises two major circuits.<ref name="Haggard, P. 2008" /> The first involving early preparatory signals ([[basal ganglia]] [[substantia nigra]] and [[striatum]]), prior intention and deliberation (medial [[prefrontal cortex]]), motor preparation/readiness potential ([[Supplementary motor area|preSMA and SMA]]), and motor execution ([[primary motor cortex]], [[spinal cord]] and [[muscle]]s). The second involving the parietal-pre-motor circuit for object-guided actions, for example grasping ([[premotor cortex]], [[primary motor cortex]], [[Postcentral gyrus|primary somatosensory cortex]], [[parietal cortex]], and back to the [[premotor cortex]]). He proposed that voluntary action involves external environment input ("when decision"), motivations/reasons for actions (early "whether decision"), task and [[action selection]] ("what decision"), a final predictive check (late "whether decision") and action execution.

Another neural model for voluntary action also involves what, when, and whether (WWW) based decisions.<ref name="BrassLynn2013">{{cite journal|last1=Brass|first1=Marcel|last2=Lynn|first2=Margaret T.|last3=Demanet|first3=Jelle|last4=Rigoni|first4=Davide|title=Imaging volition: what the brain can tell us about the will|journal=Experimental Brain Research|volume=229|issue=3|year=2013|pages=301–312|issn=0014-4819|doi=10.1007/s00221-013-3472-x|pmid=23515626|s2cid=12187338}}</ref>
The "what" component of decisions is considered a function of the [[anterior cingulate cortex]], which is involved in conflict monitoring.<ref name="MuellerBrass2007">{{cite journal|last1=Mueller|first1=Veronika A.|last2=Brass|first2=Marcel|last3=Waszak|first3=Florian|last4=Prinz|first4=Wolfgang|title=The role of the preSMA and the rostral cingulate zone in internally selected actions|journal=NeuroImage|volume=37|issue=4|year=2007|pages=1354–1361|issn=1053-8119|doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2007.06.018|pmid=17681798|s2cid=14447545}}</ref> The timing ("when") of the decisions are considered a function of the [[Supplementary motor area|preSMA and SMA]], which is involved in motor preparation.<ref name="Krieghoff2009">{{cite journal|last1=Krieghoff|first1=Veronika|title=Dissociating what and when of intentional actions|journal=Frontiers in Human Neuroscience|volume=3|page=3|year=2009|issn=1662-5161|doi=10.3389/neuro.09.003.2009|pmid=19277217|pmc=2654019|doi-access=free}}</ref>
Finally, the "whether" component is considered a function of the [[dorsum (anatomy)|dorsal]] medial [[prefrontal cortex]].<ref name="BrassLynn2013"/>

=== Prospection ===
{{Main|Prospection}}
[[Martin Seligman]] and others criticize the classical approach in science that views animals and humans as "driven by the past" and suggest instead that people and animals draw on experience to evaluate prospects they face and act accordingly. The claim is made that this purposive action includes evaluation of possibilities that have never occurred before and is experimentally verifiable.<ref name=Seligman1/><ref name=Seligman2/>

Seligman and others argue that free will and the role of subjectivity in consciousness can be better understood by taking such a "prospective" stance on cognition and that "accumulating evidence in a wide range of research suggests [this] shift in framework".<ref name=Seligman2/>

==External links==
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20150602060947/http://iai.tv/video/fate-freedom-and-neuroscience Fate, Freedom and Neuroscience] – a debate on whether neuroscience has proved that free will is an illusion by the [[Institute of Art and Ideas]] featuring Oxford neuroscientist Nayef Al-Rodhan, East End psychiatrist and broadcaster Mark Salter, and LSE philosopher Kristina Musholt debate the limits of science.
*{{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20140411072815/http://philosophyandscienceofself-control.com/ The Philosophy and Science of Self-Control]}} – an international collaborative project led by Al Mele. The project fosters collaboration between scientists and philosophers with the overarching goal of improving our understanding of self-control.
